title: Leavening Quick Breads: Steam, Soda, and Baking Powder
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
LEAVENING WITH STEAM AND AIR.--When flour is to be moistened and baked to make bread or cake, other ingredients are usually added to improve the grain, texture, and flavor. Some simple flour mixtures are lightened by steam and air. In most cases, however, more air is introduced into the mixture by using lightly beaten eggs, or by using ingredients that produce gas, on being moistened and heated.

EXPERIMENT 67: ACTION OF BAKING SODA ON SOUR MILK.--Place a teaspoonful of sour milk in a test tube and add a pinch of baking soda. Apply heat to the contents of the tube. What kind of material (solid, liquid, or gas) is indicated by the bubbling? What does this experiment teach with regard to the use of baking soda and sour milk, for lightening a mixture?

EXPERIMENT 74: EFFECT OF HOT WATER ON BAKING POWDER.--Place a small quantity of baking powder in a test tube. Add a teaspoonful of boiling water. Note the gas which bubbles from the mixture. What practical conclusions can be drawn from this experiment?

BAKING POWDER.--The usual kind of baking powder consists of an acid and an alkaline substance together with a filler (starch). When the ingredients are moistened and heated, the acid and alkaline substance react to form a gas, carbon dioxide, which lightens the mixture.

POUR BATTER.--All breads may be divided into two classes: (_a_) Quick Breads and (_b_) Yeast Breads. The former are so named because a much shorter time is required in their preparation. Quick breads are divided into several classes, depending upon the proportion of flour and moisture in the batter. A _pour batter_ is the thinnest quick bread mixture. A definite proportion cannot be stated, since the thickening quality of different flours varies.

OVEN TEMPERATURES FOR QUICK BREADS:
1. Slow oven (250-350 degrees F.) for custards and meringues.
2. Moderate oven (350-400 degrees F.) for bread, gingerbread, plain cake.
3. Hot oven (400-450 degrees F.) for Parkerhouse rolls, and Popovers.
4. Very hot oven (450-550 degrees F.) for pastry.

In preparing all quick bread mixtures, _pastry flour_ should be used. It should be sifted before measuring.
modern_explanation:
This lesson lays out the complete chemistry of quick bread leavening — one of the most practical pieces of food science for everyday baking. There are three leavening agents for quick breads, and this lesson covers all three: steam and air (from beaten eggs or vigorous mixing), baking soda with an acid (buttermilk, molasses, honey, yogurt, or cream of tartar), and baking powder (which contains both the acid and base pre-combined).

The critical principle with baking soda is that it needs an acidic ingredient to activate. Baking soda alone doesn't produce leavening gas — it needs to react with buttermilk, sour milk, molasses, honey, or brown sugar. This is why a recipe that calls for baking soda without any acidic ingredient is either using the soda purely for flavor and browning, or there's an error in the recipe. Baking powder is 'complete' — it contains its own acid, so it works with any liquid.

The oven temperature table is one of the most useful practical guides in the book. A very hot oven (450°F+) for pastry causes the fat in the dough to release steam quickly, creating flaky layers before the dough structure sets. Moderate heat for cakes gives even rising without overly rapid crust formation. These principles are still the basis of every modern baking temperature guide.
key_takeaways: Baking soda needs an acid (buttermilk, molasses, honey) to produce leavening gas — it won't work alone.
Baking powder is self-contained: mix with any liquid and it activates with heat.
Steam from moisture in batter also leavens — the more liquid, the more steam-leavening.
Different baked goods need different oven temperatures: pastry needs very hot (450°F+), cakes need moderate (350-400°F).
Sift flour before measuring to ensure consistent volume measurements.

title: Water in Cooking: Boiling, Simmering, and What They Mean
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
EXPERIMENT 6: THE DISSOLVING POWER OF WATER.--Put 1/2 teaspoonful of salt in a test tube, half fill it with water. Cover the mouth of the test tube with the thumb, then shake the tube. Do the contents become clear? Set the tube aside for a few minutes. Does the salt separate from the water? When a solid substance, by mixing with water, disappears in the water and does not separate on standing, the solid substance is _dissolved_.

SOLUTION AND DIGESTION.--The change of foods in the body from insoluble to a soluble form is one step in digestion. Some foods such as salt and certain sugars are readily dissolved. Other foods have to undergo changes before they will dissolve. Corn-starch, for example, does not dissolve in cold water. It must be changed into sugar (which is easily dissolved) in the process of digestion.

USE OF WATER IN THE BODY.--Water has many uses in the body. (_a_) It is the greatest known solvent. (_b_) It is a great carrier. Water helps carry food materials to all parts of the body; and it aids in carrying off the wastes of the body. (_c_) It assists in regulating the temperature of the body.

EXPERIMENT 8: SIMMERING AND BOILING OF WATER.--Continue to heat the water until the larger bubbles form and disappear at the surface of the water. Note the temperature. Continue to heat the water until bubbling occurs on the surface of the water. Note the temperature.

HEATING WATER.--When bubbling occurs below the surface, water is _simmering_. When the surface is in motion and steam is given off, water is _boiling_.

The loss of gases makes boiled water taste flat or insipid. This flatness can be overcome somewhat by _aerating_ the water after boiling, _i.e._ by pouring it from one vessel into another and thus mixing air with it.
modern_explanation:
Understanding the difference between simmering and boiling is one of the most practical distinctions in all of cooking. Simmering (180–210°F / 82–99°C) means occasional bubbles rise from the bottom but the surface is mostly calm. Boiling (212°F / 100°C) means the surface is churning continuously. For most soups, stews, and braises, a simmer is superior — it gently extracts flavor and softens collagen without breaking up delicate pieces of meat or vegetable.

The observation that 'a food becomes no hotter in rapidly boiling than in gently boiling water' is a fundamental truth every cook should internalize. Once water reaches 212°F, it cannot get any hotter (at normal altitude) regardless of how much heat you apply — the extra energy simply converts more water to steam. So turning up the flame on a soup does nothing to cook it faster; it just wastes fuel and evaporates your liquid.

The note about boiled water tasting flat is still valid — boiling drives off dissolved gases including oxygen and carbon dioxide, which contribute to the 'fresh' taste of water. Greer's solution of pouring the water between vessels to aerate it is still used today, though most people simply prefer cold water from the tap or filter.
key_takeaways: Simmering (bubbles rising from the bottom, calm surface) is different from boiling (churning surface).
Water cannot exceed 212°F at sea level regardless of heat applied — more heat just evaporates water faster.
Most soups, stews, and braises cook best at a simmer, not a full boil.
Boiled water loses dissolved gases and tastes flat; aerate by pouring between vessels.
Water dissolves salts and sugars readily, which is why salting cooking water and soaking dried foods works.

title: Yeast Bread: The Science of Rising and Baking
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
PROPERTIES OF YEAST.--Yeast consists of a mass of microscopic plants. When placed under proper conditions these plants grow rapidly, and in so doing they separate the sugar that exists in flour into carbon dioxide and alcohol. The carbon dioxide lightens dough. The alcohol passes off as vapor in baking.

Plants need moisture, warmth, light, and the food that is furnished by the air and soil for their growth. Yeast plants require all of these except light. Yeast thrives at a temperature of 90 degrees F. It is killed by a temperature above 130 degrees F. Hence the yeast is mixed with lukewarm water.

_Compressed yeast cakes_ which are wrapped in tinfoil and received fresh at the market every day or two are the most satisfactory to use. This yeast must be fresh for successful bread making. It is fresh when it is of a light color, is free from dark streaks, and is crumbly in texture.

GENERAL SUGGESTIONS FOR BREAD MAKING.--Use wheat bread flour, or a combination of wheat bread flour with whole wheat, or graham flour. Flour should be kept in a dry place. It is well to warm flour for bread before using.

If milk is used, scald or boil it to prevent it from souring.

It is desirable to use sufficient yeast and to subject it to desirable conditions so that the dough will rise quickly. If the rising process occupies much time, certain kinds of bacteria which may be present in the yeast or other materials may act upon the alcohol present in the risen dough and convert it into acid. This produces sour dough.

Although it is customary to allow bread to rise twice, tasty bread may be secured by one rising. By kneading bread, the larger bubbles are broken or distributed more evenly through the dough.

Loaves of bread should bake at least 1 hour at a temperature varying from 375 degrees F. to 400 degrees F. During the first 20 minutes they should rise but slightly and just begin to brown; during the second 20 minutes they should continue to brown; during the last 20 minutes they should shrink from the sides of the pan.
modern_explanation:
This lesson is still among the most accurate and useful explanations of yeast bread for home bakers. The key biological facts are exactly right: yeast is a living organism that feeds on sugars and produces carbon dioxide (which inflates the dough) and alcohol (which evaporates during baking). Kill the yeast and you get no rise; give it too much heat and it dies; give it too little and it works too slowly.

The temperature range for yeast activity (thrives around 90°F/32°C, dies above 130°F/54°C) is still accurate. This is why all bread recipes say 'lukewarm' water for activating yeast — hot water kills it, cold water makes it lethargic. The warning that slow rising allows bacteria to sour the dough is still valid, though modern bakers actually exploit this for flavorful long-fermented loaves.

The three-phase baking description is beautiful and still accurate: in the first 20 minutes, the yeast has a last burst of activity ('oven spring') before dying; in the middle phase, the crust browns through Maillard reaction; in the final phase, moisture evaporates, the loaf sets, and it shrinks away from the pan. When a bread loaf shrinks from the sides of the pan and sounds hollow when tapped on the bottom, it is done — this still holds.
key_takeaways: Mix yeast with lukewarm water (around 90°F/32°C) — too hot kills it, too cold makes it sluggish.
Carbon dioxide from yeast fermentation inflates the dough; the alcohol evaporates during baking.
Kneading distributes gas bubbles evenly throughout the dough for an even, fine crumb.
Bread is done when it shrinks from the pan sides and sounds hollow when tapped on the bottom.
Two rises produce a more even texture than one; kneading between rises is essential.

title: Candy Making: Cooking Sugar Sirups to Different Stages
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
SUGAR AND GLUCOSE.--Granulated sugar and glucose differ in taste and composition. Granulated sugar is crystalline in structure, while commercial glucose exists in the form of a heavy sirup, _i.e._ is non-crystalline in form.

In many candies, a creamy consistency is desired. This is not possible, if all the sugar of the candy exists in coarse crystalline form. Hence in the making of candy from granulated sugar, it is desirable to add glucose or sirup to granulated sugar or to change some of the crystallized sugar to a sugar which crystallizes with difficulty, _i.e._ _invert sugar_. This can be accomplished by boiling granulated sugar with acid.

COOKING SIRUPS.--Sugar and water are boiled to different degrees of temperature for making different kinds of candy. The thicker the sirup, the higher the temperature. Tests for sirups of different consistencies are:

(_a_) Thread,--when dropped from a spoon, the sirup forms a thread about two inches long (230 degrees F.).
(_b_) Soft ball,--when dropped into cold water, the sirup forms a soft ball if rolled between the fingers (236 degrees F.).
(_c_) Hard ball,--when dropped into cold water, the sirup forms a firm ball (252 degrees F.).
(_d_) Crack,--when dropped into cold water, the sirup becomes brittle (270 degrees F.).
(_e_) Hard crack,--when dropped into cold water, the sirup becomes very hard and brittle (293 degrees F.).
(_f_) Caramel,--when sugar (without addition of water) liquefies when hot and becomes very hard and brittle when cold (310 degrees F.).

NOTE.--When cane or granulated sugar is caramelized, a small quantity of an injurious substance called _furfural_ is formed. The more sugar is heated, the more of the injurious substance is produced. Also, cane sugar yields more furfural than glucose. When caramelized sugar is boiled with water, however, the furfural is expelled.
modern_explanation:
Sugar stage temperatures are the foundation of all confectionery, and Greer's stages are still taught in culinary school with virtually the same temperature ranges. At each stage, more water has evaporated from the sugar solution, increasing concentration and changing the texture of the candy when cooled.

The cold-water test (dropping a small amount of syrup into cold water and feeling the result) has been used by candy makers for centuries and is still practiced by those who don't have a thermometer. Soft ball (for fudge, fondant, pralines), hard ball (for caramels, marshmallows), crack (for taffy), and hard crack (for brittles, lollipops) are still the standard stage names used in every modern candy recipe.

The science of why glucose (corn syrup) prevents crystallization is accurate: glucose molecules interfere with the crystalline structure of sucrose, keeping candy smooth and creamy. This is why virtually all professional candy recipes call for corn syrup or glucose—it's not a cost-cutting shortcut but a deliberate textural technique.

The furfural warning is interesting from a food history perspective. Furfural is indeed formed when sugars are heated at very high temperatures—it's a flavor compound as well as a potential irritant. Modern food science doesn't treat it as a significant concern at household cooking concentrations, but the observation that it's driven off when caramel is boiled with water is accurate.
key_takeaways: Sugar syrup stages (thread, soft ball, hard ball, crack, hard crack, caramel) correspond to different candy types—learn these temperatures.
Cold-water test: dropping syrup into cold water reveals the stage—use this when no thermometer is available.
Adding glucose (corn syrup) to granulated sugar prevents crystal formation, producing smooth, creamy candy instead of grainy.
Each stage represents more water evaporation and higher sugar concentration, producing firmer candy when cooled.
Caramelized sugar forms at around 310°F; furfural (a flavor compound) forms with high heat but is harmless at cooking concentrations.

title: Sugar: Types, Behavior, and Caramelization
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
ENERGY; FUEL.--Foods which enable the body to work are termed energy-giving or fuel foods. Sugar is the first one to be considered.

THE DIGESTION OF SUGAR.--Since sugar is so readily dissolved, dissolving is an important step in the process of digestion. Some sugars, such as glucose, need no digestion in a chemical sense, and are wholesome provided their solution is not too concentrated. If sugar is eaten in large quantities there is so much dissolved sugar for the organs of digestion to take care of that the stomach and small intestines become irritated. This is especially true when candy is eaten between meals. All sweets should be eaten only in moderation and either during a meal or at its close.

SUGARS AND SIRUPS.--(_a_) _Granulated sugar_ is made either from the sugar cane or sugar beet. _Powdered sugar_ is prepared by crushing granulated sugar. Granulated sugar is 100 per cent sugar.

_Brown sugar_ is made from the cane or beet, but is not refined as much as is granulated sugar. It contains some ash and moisture.

(_b_) _Corn sirup_ is made by boiling corn-starch with an acid and then refining the product. Glucose is not as sweet as granulated sugar. At least 1 1/2 times as much corn sirup as granulated sugar is needed to produce the same sweetness.

(_c_) _Molasses_ is a by-product of cane sugar. In addition to sugar, it contains certain mineral materials such as lime.

(_e_) _Maple Sirup and Sugar_ are prepared from sap extracted from the maple tree. Maple sugar contains approximately 83 per cent of sugar, while maple sirup contains about 71 per cent.

CARAMELIZED SUGAR.--When heat is applied to granulated sugar, the latter liquefies and becomes brown in color. This brown liquid is called _caramel_. The process of making it is called _caramelization_.

When sugar is caramelizing, it reaches a high temperature. The melting point of tin is near the temperature of caramelized sugar. The enamel of granite ware is apt to chip off if subjected to great changes of temperature. Iron is not affected by the highest cooking temperature, hence it is desirable to use an iron utensil for caramelizing sugar.
modern_explanation:
This lesson gives a remarkably practical survey of all the sweeteners available in a 1920 American kitchen: granulated white sugar, powdered sugar, brown sugar, corn syrup, molasses, honey, and maple syrup. The key insight is that these are not interchangeable: they differ in sweetness level, moisture content, mineral composition, and behavior when heated.

Caramelization — the browning of sugar by heat — is explained correctly. Sugar melts at around 320°F (160°C) and then begins to brown, producing hundreds of complex flavor compounds. The practical advice to use an iron pan is still good: cast iron can handle the very high temperatures needed without cracking or melting. The warning that enamelware may chip at high temperature is accurate — it's why professional candy makers use uncoated copper or stainless steel pans.

The nutritional advice about not eating candy between meals is genuinely sound. Concentrated sugar on an empty stomach does cause rapid blood sugar spikes and can cause digestive discomfort. Eating sweets with other foods slows their absorption. The preference for molasses over pure sugar for children (because of the calcium content) reflects real nutritional science: blackstrap molasses is genuinely a good source of calcium, iron, and magnesium.
key_takeaways: Different sugars (granulated, brown, corn syrup, molasses) are not interchangeable — they differ in sweetness, moisture, and mineral content.
Caramelization requires very high heat and an iron or uncoated pan — enamelware may crack.
Corn syrup is less sweet than sugar; you need 1.5x as much to achieve the same sweetness.
Molasses contains calcium and iron not found in refined white sugar — it is nutritionally superior for children.
Eat sweets with meals, not between them — concentrated sugar alone irritates the empty digestive tract.

title: Dish-Washing and Efficiency
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
DISH-WASHING AND EFFICIENCY.--There is almost invariably a waste of effort in both the washing and the drying of dishes. This may be due to: (_a_) Poorly arranged dish-washing equipments. (_b_) Inadequate utensils for dish-washing. (_c_) Lack of forethought in preparing the dishes for washing and too many motions in washing and drying them.

Since dish-washing is one of the constant duties of housekeeping, efficiency methods, _i.e._ methods which accomplish satisfactory results with the fewest motions and in the least time, should be applied to it. The washing of dishes, invariably considered commonplace, may become an interesting problem if it is made a matter of motion study.

PREPARING DISHES FOR WASHING.--If possible, as soon as _serving dishes, i.e._ dishes used at the dining table, are soiled, scrape away bits of food from them. The scraping may be done with: (_a_) a piece of soft paper, (_b_) plate-scraper, (_c_) a knife or spoon. If it is impossible to wash dishes soon after soiling, let them soak in water until they can be washed.

_Cooking utensils_ need special care before washing, especially if they have held greasy foods. 'Oil and water do not mix!' Before putting a utensil which has held fat into the dish-water, always wipe it carefully with a piece of paper. After wiping most of the grease from a pan or kettle, the remaining fat can be entirely removed by filling the utensil with hot water and then adding washing-soda. Boil the solution a few minutes. Fat and washing-soda react and form soap.

Utensils used in cooking can generally be washed with greater efficiency if they are soaked before washing. Fill each dish or pan with water, using cold water for all utensils which have held milk, cream, eggs, flour, or starch, and hot water for all dishes having contained sugar or sirup.

ARRANGING DISHES.--Arrange dishes and all the requisite dish-washing utensils in convenient order for washing, placing all of one kind of dishes together. Also place the dishes to be washed at the _right_ of the dish-pan. Wash them and place the washed dishes at the _left_ of the pan.

WASHING AND SCOURING DISHES AND UTENSILS.--Fill the dish-pan about two thirds full of hot water. 'Soap' the water before placing the dishes in the pan; use soap-powder, a soap-holder, or a bar of soap. If the latter is used, do not allow it to remain in the water. Fill another pan about two thirds full of hot water for rinsing the dishes.

Place the dishes, a few at a time, in the dish-pan. Wash the cleanest dishes first, usually in the following order: glasses, silverware, cups, saucers, plates, large dishes, platters, cooking utensils, then the soap-dish and dish-pan. In washing decorated china, use soap sparingly. Do not wash glassware in very hot water.

DRYING DISHES AND UTENSILS.--If such dishes as plates, platters, and saucers are placed upright to drain and are rinsed with very hot water, no towel-drying is required. Glassware and silver should be dried with a soft towel. To prevent rusting, dry tin, iron, and steel utensils most thoroughly.

SUGGESTIONS FOR PERSONAL NEATNESS IN THE SCHOOL KITCHEN AND AT HOME.--For both comfort and cleanliness a washable gown should be worn in the kitchen or the gown should be well covered by an apron. It is advisable to cover the hair with a hair net or cap. The hands should be washed _before_ preparing or cooking food, and _after_ touching the hair or handkerchief.

_Clean cooking_ means _clean tasting_. This can be done by taking some of the food with the cooking spoon and then pouring it from the cooking spoon into a teaspoon. Taste from the teaspoon.
modern_explanation:
This lesson treats dish-washing as a genuine engineering problem, applying the early-twentieth-century concept of 'motion study' — the idea that you can accomplish any task faster and with less effort by thinking carefully about your movements before you begin. The advice to arrange dirty dishes on your right and clean ones on your left so you never cross your arms is ergonomics wisdom that still holds today.

The chemistry here is sound and surprisingly modern: fat and washing soda (sodium carbonate) really do react to form soap, which is why a hot soda solution cuts grease so effectively. The rule about using cold water for protein-containing dishes (eggs, milk, flour) and hot water for sugary ones is based on real biochemistry — cold water prevents protein from setting and sticking, while hot water dissolves sugar residues more readily.

The washing order — glasses first, then silver, cups and saucers, plates, then pots and pans — follows a logic that keeps the water cleaner longer. Modern automatic dishwashers have changed the process, but the principle of tackling lightly soiled items before heavily soiled ones still makes sense for hand-washing. One tip that's still excellent: wipe greasy pans with paper before washing them, to keep your dishwater clean and prevent grease from clogging the drain.

The personal hygiene advice — wash hands before cooking, cover your hair, and taste with a separate spoon — is timeless food safety practice.
key_takeaways: Use cold water to soak dishes that held eggs, milk, or starch; hot water for sugar and syrup.
Wash dishes in order from cleanest to dirtiest: glasses, silver, cups, plates, pots.
Wipe greasy pans with paper before washing to protect drain pipes and keep dishwater clean.
Hot soda water dissolves fat from pots by turning it into soap — do not use on aluminum.
Always taste from a separate spoon, not the cooking spoon, to keep food sanitary.

title: Cheese: A Concentrated Protein Food and Its Uses
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
CHEESE.--Milk contains a protein, casein, in addition to the other foodstuffs, fats, sugar, and ash. Because casein is a constituent of cheese, and because of the fat and other ingredients that may be combined with it in making cheese, the composition of different varieties of cheeses varies considerably.

From the standpoint of food value, cheese is a very valuable food. The following shows that even a small quantity of cheese is a concentrated source of protein and fat: 1 ounce of American cheese is equal in protein to 1 egg. 1 ounce of American cheese is equal in fat to 1 tablespoonful of butter.

Cheese is more difficult to digest than milk and eggs from which it is made, probably because it is such a concentrated food. It is very important that it be eaten with other foods. Cheese is usually served at the end of a meal because it is thought that the digestive organs are better prepared to receive a concentrated food after other easily digested foods have been eaten.

PRINCIPLES OF USING CHEESE IN COOKING.--Since protein (and fat and other ingredients) is the most important constituent of cheese, the principles of cooking protein-rich foods should be applied. These principles suggest that cheese should be cooked at a low temperature, and for a short time only. When too high a temperature is used, the protein of cheese, like that of egg and meat, becomes tough and stringy. This, moreover, makes the fat of the cheese separate. The resulting stringy, fat-dotted cheese product is not only unappetizing in appearance, but difficult to digest.

One of the most satisfactory methods of incorporating cheese into dishes is by using it in a White Sauce. When combined with a White Sauce in this manner, cheese is less apt to become stringy.
modern_explanation:
Greer's advice about cheese in cooking is still entirely correct and explains why your grilled cheese sandwich gets rubbery and greasy when cooked too hot for too long. Cheese protein (primarily casein) behaves like all proteins under heat: low, gentle heat melts it smoothly; high, sustained heat causes it to denature, tighten, squeeze out the fat (which pools in greasy puddles), and become chewy and unpleasant. This is still the fundamental rule of cheese cookery.

The suggestion to incorporate cheese into a white sauce (béchamel) first is sound technique. The starch in the sauce stabilizes the protein-fat emulsion and prevents separation, even at higher temperatures. This is the basis of macaroni and cheese sauce, fondue, and queso—the starch keeps everything cohesive. Modern molecular gastronomy uses sodium citrate for the same purpose: it keeps cheese proteins emulsified so they don't separate.

The comparison of cheese nutrition to eggs and butter is still useful as a quick guide: an ounce of cheddar has roughly the same protein as one large egg. The note about digestibility is interesting—cheese is concentrated and high-fat, which does slow digestion. The tradition of serving cheese after the main meal, when digestive enzymes are already active, has practical logic behind it.
key_takeaways: Cook cheese at low temperature and for a short time only—high heat makes protein tough, stringy, and causes fat to separate.
One ounce of American cheese equals roughly one egg in protein and one tablespoon of butter in fat—it's very concentrated.
Incorporating cheese into a white sauce stabilizes it and prevents the greasy-stringy texture of overheated cheese.
Cheese is most digestible when eaten with other foods, not on its own.
The stringy, oily result of overcooked cheese is both unappetizing and harder to digest.

title: Milk: An Invaluable Food and Its Care
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
MILK, AN INVALUABLE FOOD.--It has been said that there is no one food _except milk_ which cannot be eliminated from the diet. Milk is the only food for which there are no easily found substitutes. The housekeeper or one who plans the food for the family should purchase daily, if possible, a pint of milk for each adult and a quart for each child under ten years. She should see to it that this amount of milk is entirely used either as a beverage or in cooked foods. If one must economize in foods, _less should be spent for meat, and more for milk_.

The value of milk is due to the fact that it contains: (_a_) _Proteins of 'excellent quality_.' The most important protein existing in milk is called _casein_. Casein is a complete protein and is very important for growth. It precipitates when acid is added to milk. When milk sours, the sugar contained in the milk changes to an acid, and this acid causes the casein to precipitate.

(_b_) _Valuable ash_. Lime which is so essential to body-building is one of the minerals in milk. Milk is especially rich in lime.

(_c_) _Vitamines_. These are substances contained only in certain foods. They are essential for maintaining life and health. Milk is rich in these indispensable materials.

CARE OF MILK.--Milk is one of the foods that require the greatest care. It affords ideal conditions for the growth of microscopic vegetable organisms, called _bacteria_. Milk can be kept reasonably free from bacteria by: (_a_) Perfect cleanliness on the dairy farm. (_b_) Cooling it immediately after being drawn from the cow, and by keeping it cool. (_c_) Placing it in sterilized utensils. (_d_) Covering it, thus keeping it free from dust.

Utensils for holding milk should be of glass, earthenware, or smooth, bright tin. They should be washed, scalded, or even better, boiled, and placed in the sun for two or three hours. Because milk readily absorbs odors and flavors, it should be kept away from any substance having a strong odor or flavor.

WHIPPING CREAM.--Whipping cream is done most successfully when the cream is cold and kept cold, _i.e._ surrounded with ice water during the beating process. Use thick cream (containing 20 per cent or more of fat) from 12 to 24 hours old and have it very cold; it will then whip quickly.
modern_explanation:
Greer's declaration that milk 'is the only food for which there are no easily found substitutes' reflects the nutritional consensus of her era—and it held up for a long time. Milk's combination of complete protein, calcium, fat-soluble vitamins, and carbohydrate in one liquid food is genuinely unique. Today we have oat milk, almond milk, and soy milk, but none of them replicate the precise nutritional profile of cow's milk without fortification.

The science of why milk sours is clearly explained: bacteria convert milk sugar (lactose) to lactic acid, which then causes the casein protein to precipitate (curdle). This is the same process behind yogurt, cheese, and buttermilk—all controlled applications of exactly what happens when milk 'goes off.' Understanding this chemistry helps cooks recognize that sour milk isn't necessarily spoiled milk; it can be used in baking where the acid reacts with baking soda as a leavening agent.

The milk safety advice is excellent: keep it cold, use clean containers, cover it to prevent odor absorption, and scald or boil utensils. The sun exposure advice works because UV light destroys surface bacteria—an early form of UV sterilization. The milk-keeps-odors warning is still relevant: milk stored near strong-smelling foods (onions, fish) in an uncovered refrigerator will absorb those flavors.

The whipping cream advice is still entirely correct: cold cream whips better because the fat molecules are more stable when chilled. Modern bakers chill both the cream and the bowl for the best results.
key_takeaways: Milk's combination of complete protein, calcium, and vitamins makes it the most nutritionally complete single food.
Milk sours when bacteria convert lactose to lactic acid, curdling the casein—sour milk is useful in baking with baking soda.
Keep milk covered and away from strong-smelling foods; it readily absorbs odors and flavors.
Milk utensils should be scalded or boiled and sun-dried to kill surface bacteria.
Cold cream (20%+ fat, at least 12–24 hours old) whips best; keep it over ice water during beating.

title: Eggs: Protein Coagulation and Proper Cooking Temperature
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
PROTEIN, A BODY-BUILDER AND REPAIRER.--There is another great class of foods, or foodstuffs--those included under the term _protein_--that not only give energy to the body but also build up or promote growth and repair it or support life. Milk, eggs, cheese, meat, and fish contain complete proteins, while beans, peas, gelatine, and certain cereals contain incomplete proteins.

EXPERIMENT 37: THE COAGULATION OF EGG WHITE.--Put the white of an egg in a dish and break the membranes by cutting with a pair of scissors. Then place a small quantity of the white of egg in a test tube. Apply heat. Into what form is the liquid egg white changed by heat? When eggs are cooked, the protein in the white called _albumin_ stiffens or _coagulates_. The yolk also contains a kind of protein which coagulates when heated.

EXPERIMENT 39: TEMPERATURE AT WHICH EGGS COAGULATE.--Place a teaspoonful of white of egg in a test tube. Insert a thermometer in the test tube and place the test tube in a beaker of water. Heat the water _gradually_. Note and record: (_a_) Temperature at which coagulation first appears. (_b_) Temperature at which the egg white is entirely coagulated. Has the water reached the boiling point when the egg white has entirely coagulated? What application can you draw from this as to the temperature of the water in which eggs may be cooked?

EXPERIMENT 40: COMPARISON OF COOKED AND BOILED EGGS.--Remove _at once_ about half of the coagulated egg from the test tube. Continue to heat the remainder of the egg in the test tube, allowing the water to boil a few minutes. Compare it with the egg cooked below the boiling point of water. Which is more tender?

DIGESTIBILITY AND PALATABILITY OF EGGS.--The experiments of this lesson show that eggs cooked at simmering temperature are more tender than those cooked at boiling temperature. Eggs cooked below the boiling temperature will digest in a little less time than those cooked in boiling water.

SOFT-COOKED EGGS
Place eggs in enough boiling water to cover. Remove from the fire, cover, and allow to stand from 5 to 8 minutes.

HARD-COOKED EGGS.--Place eggs in cold water and heat the water gradually until it reaches the boiling point. Remove from the fire at once; cover and place on the back of range, or in a warm place, for 20 minutes. Plunge into cold water, so that the shells may be removed easily.
modern_explanation:
This is one of the most scientifically accurate and practically useful lessons in the book. Greer's key insight — that eggs coagulate completely below the boiling point of water — is modern egg cookery in a nutshell. Egg white proteins begin to set around 144°F (62°C) and are fully cooked by 180°F (82°C), well before the 212°F (100°C) of boiling water. This means you never need to boil eggs — you can cook them perfectly in hot water that you've removed from heat.

The resulting texture is the key difference: eggs cooked below boiling are tender, silky, and evenly set throughout. Eggs that have been boiling hard for 10 minutes have a rubbery white, a grainy yolk, and often that green ring around the yolk (caused by sulfur and iron compounds reacting at high temperature). The gentle method produces a much better egg.

Greer's method for hard-cooked eggs — bring to boil then remove from heat and let stand covered 20 minutes — is exactly the technique recommended today by food scientists and published in the New York Times and Serious Eats. The cold water plunge immediately after cooking prevents the green ring and makes peeling easier. This lesson, written in 1920, could be copy-pasted into any modern cooking blog.
key_takeaways: Eggs coagulate (set) at temperatures well below boiling — you do not need boiling water to cook eggs.
For perfect hard-cooked eggs: bring to boil, remove from heat, cover and rest 20 minutes, then plunge in cold water.
Eggs cooked below boiling are more tender and silky; eggs 'boiled' hard become rubbery and grainy.
Always cook eggs in a covered pan off heat — sustained boiling makes them tough.
Separate eggs should each be broken into a small dish before combining, to catch any spoiled egg early.

title: Fuels, Combustion, and Heat for Cooking
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
FUEL.--In order to cook foods, heat in some form must be applied. This heat is obtained usually by burning some substance. Thus the first requisite for obtaining heat is something to burn, _i.e._ a fuel. The fuels commonly used in households are,--wood, coal, kerosene, and gas. Although electricity is not a fuel, its use in cooking is so well established that it should be mentioned as a source of heat.

HEAT; KINDLING TEMPERATURE.--There are fuel substances everywhere,--paper, cloth, wood, etc. These materials do not burn unless heated; even gas does not burn by simply turning on the stopcock. All fuels must be heated before they will burn. When one thinks of the ease with which paper 'catches fire' and of the difficulty of making hard coal burn, it becomes evident that some substances require only a small amount of heat before they will burn, while others require much heat. The temperature to which a substance must be heated in order to burn and continue to burn is called the _kindling temperature_ of that substance.

DRAFT; OXYGEN.--If a blanket is thrown upon a burning stick of wood, the wood soon ceases to burn. The wood stops burning because the oxygen of the air is excluded from it. _The act of burning,_ i.e. _combustion, is the union of any substance with oxygen, with the result that heat and light are produced._ We have learned that a fuel cannot unite with oxygen until heated to a certain temperature. And, no matter how hot it is, the fuel will not burn unless it unites with oxygen. Oxygen, then, is the third requisite for combustion. The necessity for a draft, _i.e._ a continuous supply of fresh air which furnishes oxygen, is shown by experiment.

The three requisites for combustion are: (1) a fuel, (2) heat above the kindling temperature, and (3) oxygen (from a continuous draft of air).
modern_explanation:
Before gas and electric stoves became standard, every cook needed to understand fire: what makes it start, what keeps it burning, and what puts it out. Greer teaches the three legs of combustion — fuel, heat, and oxygen — in the context of practical kitchen work. These principles are genuinely still useful: they explain why a covered pan puts out a grease fire (oxygen is cut off), why you need a chimney damper open when lighting a fire (draft), and why charcoal is harder to light than paper.

The concept of kindling temperature — the minimum heat needed for a fuel to sustain burning — explains the sequence of fire-building: paper first (low kindling temp), then thin wood, then heavy wood or coal. Each successive fuel has a higher kindling temperature and needs the heat from the previous one to get started. This is still exactly how you build a campfire or light a wood stove.

Today, most home cooks use gas or electricity and never build a fire. But understanding combustion is still helpful for anyone who uses a charcoal grill, a wood-fired oven, or a fireplace. And the lesson that smothering a fire — cutting off its oxygen — is the most effective way to extinguish it remains the foundation of fire safety training.
key_takeaways: The three requirements for combustion are fuel, sufficient heat (above kindling temperature), and oxygen.
Each fuel has a different kindling temperature — paper lights first, then wood, then coal.
A draft (airflow) is essential for sustained burning, as it supplies fresh oxygen.
Covering a fire cuts off oxygen and extinguishes it — the basis of fire safety.
Gas does not burn without being lit; electricity is a heat source, not a fuel.

title: Method of Freezing: Ice Creams and Ices
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
EXPERIMENT 79: MIXTURES FOR FREEZING.--Prepare 2 cupfuls of cracked ice. Place 1 cupful of the cracked ice in each of two bowls. To one bowl of ice add 1/3 cupful of rock salt, and mix thoroughly. Insert thermometers into both bowls and note temperature. What effect does the salt have upon the temperature of the ice?

FREEZING WITH ICE AND SALT.--When a solid substance is changed to a liquid, heat is absorbed from surrounding materials. Salt makes ice liquefy at a lower temperature, thus absorbing more heat from its surroundings. Since foods must be cooled to freeze them, a mixture of ice and salt rather than ice alone is used in freezing.

CONDUCTORS OF HEAT.--Which is a better conductor of heat, wood or metal? Most freezers consist of an inner can of metal and an outer bucket of wood. Metal conducts heat rapidly from the food to the ice; wood insulates the outside of the freezer from the room's warmth.

PREPARING AND PACKING THE FREEZER.--For _freezing_ ice creams and most ices use _three_ parts of cracked ice to _one_ of rock salt. If ice of coarse grain is desired, use a greater quantity of salt.

FREEZING.--If a dessert of fine texture is desired, turn the crank slowly and steadily until the mixture is rather stiff, then turn more rapidly. When the mixture is frozen, remove the ice and salt around the top of the can; wipe the cover and top; uncover; and remove the dasher. Then stir the frozen mixture thoroughly; place thin paper or paraffin paper over the can; cover; place a cork in the hole of the cover. Drain off all the water which has collected in freezing; repack the freezer with ice and salt mixture; cover with carpet, blanket, or newspapers; and allow to stand in a cold place several hours.
modern_explanation:
Before electric freezers, making ice cream required understanding the chemistry of salt and ice. Pure ice melts at exactly 32°F (0°C). When salt is added, it dissolves in the thin film of water on the ice surface, creating a brine with a lower freezing point. To freeze from liquid to solid, the brine pulls heat from the surrounding ice and from the cream in the inner container, dropping the temperature well below 32°F — cold enough to freeze the cream.

This is the same chemistry behind why roads are salted in winter. The 3-to-1 ratio of ice to rock salt still produces a temperature of about 25°F (-4°C), which is cold enough to freeze standard ice cream but not so cold that it freezes too quickly and creates a coarse icy texture. The slow-then-fast cranking technique is also still valid: slow stirring initially breaks up large ice crystals before they fully form, while fast churning at the end incorporates air for a lighter texture.

The double-wall design of the freezer (metal inner can, wooden outer bucket) is exactly the same logic as a modern ice cream maker: the metal conducts cold from the ice mixture into the cream, while the wood insulates the whole assembly from room temperature, preventing the ice-and-salt bath from warming too quickly.
key_takeaways: Salt lowers the freezing point of ice, creating a mixture cold enough to freeze cream.
Use 3 parts cracked ice to 1 part rock salt for a working freezing temperature around 25°F.
Stir slowly at first to prevent large ice crystals; churn faster at the end to incorporate air.
The metal inner can conducts cold to the cream; the wooden outer insulates from room temperature.
After freezing, pack the ice cream and let it 'ripen' for several hours for best texture.

title: Measurements and the Need for Accuracy
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
EXPERIMENT 1: MEASUREMENT EQUIVALENTS.--In measuring solid materials with teaspoon, tablespoon, or standard measuring cup, fill the measuring utensil with the material and then 'level' it with a knife.

Use both water and flour or sugar for the following measurements:
(_a_) Find the number of teaspoonfuls in one tablespoonful
(_b_) Find the number of tablespoonfuls in one cup
(_c_) Find the number of cupfuls in one pint

Half a spoonful is obtained by dividing through the middle lengthwise. A quarter of a spoonful is obtained by dividing a half crosswise. An eighth of a spoonful is obtained by dividing a quarter diagonally. A third of a spoonful is obtained by dividing twice crosswise.

NEED OF ACCURACY--When learning to cook, it is necessary to measure all ingredients with exactness. Experienced cooks can measure some ingredients for certain purposes quite satisfactorily 'by eye'. The result is satisfactory, however, only when the cook has established her own standards of measurements by much practice. Even then many housewives are not _sure_ of success. For certain foods the ingredients should always be measured accurately, no matter how skilful the cook.

Many foods, especially fats, are more conveniently weighed than measured. Kitchen scales are a useful equipment for cooking.

The amateur should, however, train her eye to approximate measurements. She should learn to estimate the size of saucepans and other cooking utensils, and also of serving dishes. Measure by cupfuls the capacity of several utensils in constant use and thus establish a few standards of measurement.

Also it is well to be on the alert to learn the proper quantity of food to buy at market, and the proper quantity of food to cook for a stated number of persons. A thrifty housekeeper would not buy enough lettuce or spinach for ten people when there were only six to be served. In the school kitchen always note the quantity of the materials used, and then observe the quantity of the finished product.
modern_explanation:
This lesson lays out the American volumetric measuring system as it was standardized in the early twentieth century — the same system used in almost every U.S. recipe today. Three teaspoons to a tablespoon, sixteen tablespoons to a cup, two cups to a pint — these equivalences are so ingrained now that we rarely think about them, but they had to be consciously learned and adopted.

Greer's advice to 'level' spoon measures with a knife is the definitive rule for accurate baking and remains best practice today. A heaped tablespoon of baking powder can hold nearly twice as much as a level one, which is the difference between a cake that rises beautifully and one that has a bitter, soapy flavor. The book also notes that fats are best weighed rather than measured — professional bakers use a scale for butter for exactly this reason.

There's wonderful practical wisdom here about learning your own equipment. Greer suggests measuring the capacity of the pots and pans you actually use so that you can estimate quantities without fetching a measuring cup every time. This is how confident cooks work — they know that their favorite saucepan holds exactly 1.5 quarts, or that their coffee mug is roughly 1.5 cups.
key_takeaways: Always level dry ingredients with the back of a straight knife for consistent results.
3 teaspoons = 1 tablespoon; 16 tablespoons = 1 cup; 2 cups = 1 pint.
Fats like butter are best weighed rather than measured by volume.
Learn the capacity of your own pots and pans so you can estimate confidently.
Measuring accurately is essential for baking; more experienced cooks can develop an eye for other dishes.

title: Dining Room Service and Table Setting
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
LESSON XXX: DINING ROOM SERVICE.--A dining table with carefully laid covers is always inviting. Graceful _serving_ of food at such a table is an art. The ability to serve food in an attractive way is an accomplishment that no girl should fail to acquire.

For an afternoon tea, the beverage may be poured in the kitchen and carried into the dining room or the other room where the guests are assembled, or it may be poured in the dining room in the presence of the guests.

Those who are serving the tea should be watchful and note when the guests have drunk their tea and relieve them of cup and plate. They should also replenish the teapot, and see that the one pouring the tea has all the materials and dishes needed.

HOW TO HOLD THE KNIFE AND FORK.--The knife is held in the right hand, the handle in the palm of the hand, the forefinger placed on the back of the blade. The fork is usually held in the same manner as the knife. When using the fork with the knife for cutting, the fork is held with tines down; in carrying food to the mouth, the tines may be turned up.

THE TEASPOON SHOULD REST ON THE SAUCER when not in use.

HOW TO HOLD THE SOUP SPOON.--Hold the spoon in the right hand; push the spoon away from you, filling the spoon at the side rather than at the tip.

EFFICIENCY IN TABLE SETTING: When you are to set the table of the evening meal for one week, keep in mind: (1) To make as few trips as possible from the cupboard to the dining table. (2) To plan the entire number of dishes, knives, forks, spoons, and other things needed during the meal, and then place these on the dining table or other suitable place where they may be conveniently obtained when the meal is being served.
modern_explanation:
Table service was considered a genuine life skill in the early twentieth century, taught alongside cooking because a meal is only complete when it's served well. The specific etiquette rules Greer describes — spoon on the saucer, soup spoon pushed away, knife in right hand — are still considered standard Western table manners, even though they're followed less formally today.

The efficiency insight — plan all the dishes you'll need before starting a single trip, to minimize kitchen-to-table travel — is essentially the restaurant concept of mise en place applied to the home dining room. Setting the table well in advance, with everything you'll need already in place, prevents the rushed mid-meal scramble for a forgotten fork or extra napkin.

The idea of paying attention to guests and anticipating their needs while serving tea or a meal reflects a genuine hospitality philosophy: a good host makes everyone comfortable without drawing attention to the service itself. This principle is still the basis of professional restaurant service training.
key_takeaways: Plan your entire table setting before making any trips — it saves steps and ensures nothing is forgotten.
The teaspoon rests on the saucer, not in the cup, when not in use.
Soup spoon is pushed away from you when filling; tea is poured and cups removed without interrupting conversation.
A well-set table before guests arrive shows hospitality and makes the meal flow smoothly.
Service at table should be anticipatory — watch for empty cups, cleared plates, and needs before they're voiced.

title: Cooking Green Vegetables: Saving the Minerals
source_book: School and Home Cooking
original_text:
FRESH VEGETABLES.--It was mentioned above that fresh vegetables are one of the most valuable food sources of ash. Fresh vegetables contain not only the foodstuff ash, but water. Most fresh vegetables contain from 75 to 90 per cent of water. In addition to these two foodstuffs, vegetables contain _cellulose_. The latter is a fibrous substance which forms for the most part the skins and interior framework of vegetables and fruits.

SUGGESTIONS FOR COOKING GREEN VEGETABLES.--A most important point to consider in the cooking of vegetables is the saving of the minerals. This can be accomplished in several ways:
1. Cooking in water with their skins.
2. Cooking in water and using the water which must be drained away after cooking for sauces and soups.
3. Cooking in such a small quantity of water that none needs to be drained away after cooking.
4. Cooking in steam.
5. Cooking in the oven by means of dry heat.

COOKING VEGETABLES IN WATER.--Water in which vegetables are cooked should be salted. Use 1 teaspoonful of salt for each quart of water. The water should be _boiling_ when the vegetables are added and should be kept boiling _gently_ during the entire cooking. Rapidly boiling water wears off the edges of vegetables and breaks them.

The water in which vegetables are cooked is called _vegetable stock_. When vegetables are pared or scraped before cooking in water, the stock should be utilized in making vegetable sauces.

TIME FOR COOKING FRESH VEGETABLES IN WATER.--It is not possible to state just how long a vegetable will be required to cook in water. The time varies with the kind of vegetable, its size, and age. Usually the older a vegetable, the longer the time required for cooking. Young vegetables, especially green corn and tender cabbage, may be spoiled by too long cooking. Judging by appearance and using the fork or knitting needle is the most reliable test.

PARING VEGETABLES.--If the outside skin of a vegetable is removed, it should be pared as thin as possible. The covering of the carrot and new potato is so thin that it can be removed by scraping, thereby saving the valuable nutritive substances just beneath the skin.
modern_explanation:
This is one of the most nutritionally-informed cooking lessons in the book. Greer recognized in 1920 what modern food science confirms: minerals and vitamins in vegetables dissolve into the cooking water and are lost when you pour that water down the drain. Her five strategies for minimizing this loss are still exactly right: cook with skins on, steam rather than boil, use the minimum amount of water, save and use the cooking liquid, or roast with dry heat.

The advice to use boiling (not cold) water and to keep it at a gentle rather than rolling boil is important for color and texture as well as nutrition. Dropping vegetables into already-boiling water quickly destroys enzymes that cause loss of color and vitamin C. A gentle boil — not a rolling one — keeps vegetables from being physically battered apart while they cook.

The instruction to pare as thinly as possible is backed by real nutrition science: many vegetables, including potatoes and carrots, have their highest concentration of minerals and vitamins immediately beneath the skin. Modern nutritionists echo this advice. Using vegetable cooking water in soups and sauces is still one of the simplest ways to recover nutrients you'd otherwise discard.
key_takeaways: Save vegetable cooking water for soups and sauces — it's full of dissolved minerals.
Steam or roast vegetables to retain the most nutrients; if boiling, use minimal water.
Drop vegetables into already-boiling (not cold) water to deactivate enzymes and preserve color.
Keep the boil gentle to preserve vegetable structure — a rolling boil bashes them to pieces.
Pare vegetables as thinly as possible; the most nutrient-dense layer is just under the skin.

title: Muffins, Biscuit, Cake, and Pastry: Leavening and Proportions
source_book: Foods and Household Management: A Textbook of the Household Arts
original_text:
Wheat flour is the important material in this group, but muffins and biscuit may be varied by the use of corn meal, rye, and Graham flour, and cooked cereals may also be utilized. The ingredients are flour, salt, a liquid, sometimes a fat, eggs, and sugar.

=Leavening agents.=——The batter, or dough, is leavened by introducing into it air or a gas that expands when heated in the oven, thus making the whole more porous and larger in bulk.
_Air._——This is introduced into the batter by beating, or by beating air into the white of egg and stirring the beaten white into the batter.
_Steam._——The water in the batter turns to steam in the oven, and as it expands it assists in the leavening of the mass.
_Carbon dioxide gas._——This is introduced in three ways: (1) By using an acid with a carbonate. (2) By yeast fermentation. (3) By machinery (a commercial process).

=The proportions of the main ingredients.=——A 'pour batter' is liquid enough to pour, and a 'dough batter' soft enough to drop from a spoon; a 'soft dough' is next in grade, and 'dough' is the stiffest of all.

This general rule may be given: As the quantity of butter is increased, the batter must increase in stiffness, and there must be either less liquid or more flour.

=Methods of mixing.=——(1) _For popovers, griddlecakes, muffins, and plain cake._ Sift together the dry ingredients. Beat the eggs, without separating, and stir the eggs and milk together. Pour the liquid gradually into the flour, first stirring, then beating. Melt the butter, and beat it into the batter.

(2) _Biscuits and shortcakes._ Sift together the dry ingredients. Cut in or chop in the butter. Add the wetting slowly.

(3) _A richer, fine-grained butter cake._ Sift together the dry ingredients. Cream the butter, and beat in the sugar. Beat the whites and yolks of the eggs separately. Beat the yolks into the creamed butter and sugar. Add the flour and milk alternately. Fold in the beaten whites lightly and do not beat again.

=Baking.=——This is a science and an art that requires much practice. See that the oven is ready before the mixing begins. We shall not be able to bake accurately until our ovens are equipped with thermometers.
modern_explanation:
This chapter is a masterclass in baking fundamentals that is still as relevant as it was in 1914. The three leavening methods—air, steam, and carbon dioxide (from baking soda/powder or yeast)—are exactly what modern baking science teaches. Understanding which method you're relying on changes everything about how you mix and handle a batter.

The 'pour batter to stiff dough' spectrum is the key to understanding every bread and cake recipe. Popovers and pancakes are pour batters (thin, air-leavened); muffins are drop batters; biscuits are soft doughs. The rule that more fat requires less liquid (because fat acts as a liquid in the oven) explains why pound cake—with a pound of butter—needs no liquid other than eggs. This is still the way baking teachers explain proportions.

The three mixing methods describe what we now call the 'muffin method,' the 'biscuit method,' and the 'creaming method.' Each produces a different texture: tender-crumbed muffins, flaky biscuits, and fine-grained layer cakes. Following the wrong method (like over-developing gluten by over-mixing muffins) is the most common baking mistake, and these authors understood it perfectly.

Their note that 'we shall not be able to bake accurately until our ovens are equipped with thermometers' is historically poignant—oven thermometers were a luxury in 1914. Every cook had to use the hand-test or the paper-browning test to estimate oven temperature. Modern cooks with calibrated ovens are very lucky.
key_takeaways: The three leavening agents are air (beaten in), steam (water turning to vapor), and carbon dioxide (from soda/yeast).
Batter consistency ranges from 'pour batter' (thin) to stiff dough—each type requires different handling.
More fat in a recipe means less liquid is needed, because fat functions as a liquid in the oven.
Use the muffin method (liquid into flour) for quick breads; the biscuit method (cut fat into flour) for flaky results; the creaming method for fine-grained cakes.
Do not beat batter after folding in egg whites—this deflates the air you just incorporated.

title: Yeast Bread: Flour, Fermentation, and Bread Making
source_book: Foods and Household Management: A Textbook of the Household Arts
original_text:
Wheat flour, the standard material for bread, contains two proteins which together make _gluten_. These proteins, glutenin and gliadin, are insoluble in water, but when kneaded with water they form the elastic, tough, somewhat sticky substance, gluten. The gluten, in forming a mesh-work throughout the dough, holds it together, and when the yeast forms gas bubbles, they are caught in this net-work and held, and the dough 'rises.'

Yeast is a plant. One form of yeast floats in the air, and lies upon the surface of fruit. All cultivated yeast has been derived from wild yeast. In old-fashioned ways of bread-making no yeast was introduced, a soft dough being left in a warm place to ferment naturally, the yeast cells probably being present in the flour.

In recipes where three risings are called for one may be omitted, if time is short. It is impossible in an ordinary school period to complete the entire bread-making lesson. The dough can be prepared and started rising before the lesson, or the baking may be done in a subsequent period.

If yeast is in good condition, it has little effect on the flavor of the bread. Bread of good flavor can be made from one rising. Overbaking dries out the bread and produces a hard crust. Underbaking leaves a heavy doughy interior. The bread must be removed from the pans as soon as it is done, or the steam condenses and softens the lower crust. The loaves are cooled on a rack, that steam may escape from all surfaces.
modern_explanation:
The science of gluten formation is the key to understanding all bread making, and Kinne and Cooley explain it accurately: two proteins in wheat flour—glutenin and gliadin—combine when hydrated and kneaded to form gluten, an elastic protein network. It's this network that traps the carbon dioxide produced by yeast, causing the dough to rise. No gluten network, no rise. It's also why gluten-free bread requires completely different structural ingredients like xanthan gum or psyllium husk.

The observation that wild yeast 'floats in the air and lies upon the surface of fruit' is the basis of all sourdough bread making. Before commercial yeast was available, bakers captured wild yeasts by leaving flour-and-water mixtures exposed to the air. This 'starter' or 'levain' could be kept alive for generations by regular feeding. Sourdough has seen a major revival in modern baking.

The practical bread-making advice here is still sound: remove loaves from pans immediately after baking to prevent the crust from steaming soft, and cool on a rack. Overbaking dries the crumb; underbaking leaves a doughy interior. The test for a done loaf—tapping the bottom, which sounds hollow when fully baked—is still the standard check before a thermometer became available.
key_takeaways: Gluten is formed when glutenin and gliadin proteins in wheat flour are hydrated and kneaded—this mesh traps gas and makes bread rise.
Wild yeast exists on the surface of fruit and in the air—the basis of sourdough bread making.
Remove bread from pans immediately after baking to prevent condensation softening the bottom crust.
Overbaking dries the crumb; underbaking leaves a heavy doughy interior.
Cool bread on a rack so steam can escape from all surfaces, preventing sogginess.

title: The Fats and the Sugars: Their Effect of Heat and Uses in Cookery
source_book: Foods and Household Management: A Textbook of the Household Arts
original_text:
=Fats.=——Solid fats are liquefied by heat, and freed from the tissue that contains them in animal fats like suet. When a fat begins to smoke with heat, a chemical change is taking place. If intense heat is continued, all the hydrogen and oxygen are driven off and pure carbon remains. When the fat is 'brown,' giving the flavor we like, a part of the oxygen and hydrogen have been driven off. The 'boiling' of fat in a kettle is ordinarily due to the boiling of the water contained in the fat.

=Sugar.=——Sugar first melts with heat, then begins to decompose, giving off water. This is also a stage on the road to pure carbon. Caramel, a familiar flavor, is sugar in the brown stage, with the water partly driven off.

The art in applying intense heat to fat, starch, and sugar is to know the stopping point,——to reach the 'brown taste' and stop short of the 'burnt taste.'

_In buying sugar_ it is economy to purchase granulated in large quantities. Brown sugar is moist and apt to pack, so is more conveniently sold in smaller quantities. Powdered sugar is used in confections and fine desserts. Confectioner's sugar is powdered and starch is added to it to prevent its caking. The natural unrefined sugars, like maple sugar and molasses, contain mineral matter not found in refined sugar, and have special flavors.

The _stages of cooking sugar_ are important to know for candy making:
Thread,——230°F. Soft ball,——238°F. Hard ball,——250°F. Crack,——270°–290°F. Hard crack,——300°F. Caramel,——310°F–338°F.

Stirring the mixture while it boils or before it has cooled sufficiently will result in the formation of cane sugar crystals, and the fondant will be harsh and rough.
modern_explanation:
Fat and sugar are the most misunderstood ingredients in the kitchen—and this chapter explains their behavior in heat with pleasing clarity. Fats have a 'smoke point': the temperature at which they start to break down chemically and produce acrid-smelling acrolein. Every fat has a different smoke point (butter is lower than refined coconut oil; clarified butter higher than whole butter), and cooking beyond it produces unpleasant flavors and potentially harmful compounds. The authors capture this beautifully: there's a window between 'pleasant brown' and 'burnt,' and skill is knowing where it is.

Sugar stages are still the foundation of candy making. The thread/soft ball/hard ball/crack/caramel progression corresponds to increasing water evaporation as sugar is heated. Each stage reflects the concentration of sugar in the solution. The cold-water test described here (dropping sugar syrup into cold water to assess its pliability) is still used by candy-makers without a thermometer, though thermometers are more precise. The 'don't stir while boiling' rule for caramel and fondant is still essential—stirring introduces crystals that cause the whole batch to seize up.

Their note about confectioner's sugar having added starch is still true today—that fine powdered sugar has cornstarch mixed in (usually about 3%) to prevent caking. It's why confectioner's sugar can't be substituted for regular sugar in cooking—the starch affects texture and can cause sauces to thicken unexpectedly.
key_takeaways: Every fat has a smoke point—the temperature where chemical breakdown starts producing acrid flavors. Stay below it.
Sugar progresses through stages as water evaporates: thread, soft ball, hard ball, crack, caramel.
The art of cooking with sugar and fat is knowing the stopping point—'brown taste' vs 'burnt taste.'
Do not stir candy syrup while boiling—it introduces crystals that cause the batch to seize.
Confectioner's sugar contains added cornstarch and cannot substitute directly for granulated sugar in cooking.

title: Eggs, Milk, and Cheese: Principles of Preparation
source_book: Foods and Household Management: A Textbook of the Household Arts
original_text:
If a tender, jellylike consistency is wanted, cook the egg below the boiling point of water. It is the easier and quicker method.

If you can buy eggs by the crate direct from the poultryman, this is a saving of money. The local market will then take your money for a better quality, and a higher profit will go to the middleman. An egg is entirely fresh when laid. The 'freshness' diminishes day by day, as the water in the egg gradually evaporates through the pores of the shell. An old egg will float when put in water; a fresh one will sink. To make the freshness test, drop the egg gently into a large bowl of water.

=Clean milk.=——The danger of infection from bacteria is greatest in the case of milk, because it is always taken at a temperature that is favorable for the growth of bacteria. Moreover, it is a most favorable medium for bacterial growth. The dairy cow is sometimes affected with a contagious disease, and when this is the case, the bacteria of that disease pass into the milk. In the last few years the question of pasteurization has been studied, and it has been found practicable to heat milk to 145° F. and hold it at this temperature for thirty minutes; or to heat it rapidly to 160° F. This process kills most of the bacteria present, and is called _pasteurization_ from Pasteur, who worked out the principles of sterilization by heat.

=General directions for cooking eggs.=——The temperature at which the egg albumin coagulates is below the boiling point of water, and is about 160° F. Hence, if you cook an egg in water, the temperature of the water should not reach the boiling point. To cook a 'boiled' egg, bring the water to the boiling point, remove it from the fire, and let the egg stay in it for from five to eight minutes, depending upon the result you wish.
modern_explanation:
One of the most important kitchen science lessons of the era: eggs should never actually boil. Egg albumin (the white) coagulates at around 160°F, which is well below the 212°F boiling point of water. Boiling an egg makes the white tough and rubbery; the ideal 'hard-cooked' egg is made by bringing water to a boil, removing from heat, and letting the egg sit covered in the hot water. This technique—which Kinne and Cooley describe precisely—is still taught by culinary schools today.

Their discussion of milk safety is prescient. Writing in 1914, they explain pasteurization by name, credit Pasteur, give the correct temperatures (145°F for 30 minutes, or 160°F quickly), and explain the mechanism. Raw milk from infected cows could carry tuberculosis, typhoid, and scarlet fever. Pasteurization was controversial at the time; many people opposed it as 'interfering with nature.' The authors are admirably clear that it saves lives.

The egg freshness test—floating versus sinking in water—is still valid and still used. A fresh egg is dense with little air; as it ages, water evaporates through the porous shell and the air cell grows, making the egg buoyant. An egg that stands upright (not floating, not lying flat) is older but still edible; one that floats should be discarded. The authors were teaching kitchen food-safety practices that would be at home in any modern food-handler's certification course.
key_takeaways: Never boil eggs at a rolling boil—egg albumin coagulates at 160°F, well below boiling, so the residual heat method gives the best texture.
A fresh egg sinks flat; an older egg stands upright; a very old egg floats—this test is still reliable today.
Pasteurization (145°F for 30 minutes, or 160°F quickly) kills most harmful bacteria in milk without destroying nutrients.
Milk is an especially hospitable medium for bacterial growth and must be kept cold and clean.
Cooking eggs below boiling produces a more tender, uniform result than rapid boiling.

title: Cereal Products: Principles of Preparation
source_book: Foods and Household Management: A Textbook of the Household Arts
original_text:
Cereal grains are the seeds of grasses, and the six chief varieties are wheat, corn, oats, rice, rye, and barley. In our markets, the grain is sold either whole, or cracked, or ground to a greater or less degree of fineness, or made into flakes by rolling the softened grain flat. These many forms of one grain, and the varieties of each grain used, make a great diversity in the cereal section of the grocery.

Cereals are our cheapest and most important food. They are rich in starch and in protein, and contain some fat and mineral matter.

Starch occurs in the form of granules. See Fig. 39. In boiling water, the granule expands and finally bursts, and frees the content, the pure starch, and the whole mass thickens.

Boiled with an acid the starch is changed to dextrin, a substance resembling a gum, and the mixture becomes thin; and this process continued changes the dextrin to dextrose.

With intense 'dry' heat, as in toasting, the granule expands and opens, and the contents change to dextrin. Continued heat reduces the starch to pure carbon. The brown color and pleasant flavor in toast are a stage on the road to carbon.

In experimenting with a cereal at home it is well to try the proportion of two tablespoonfuls of cereal to a cup of liquid, using one teaspoonful of salt to a quart of water, and cooking for twenty minutes as a minimum for most cereals. The double boiler is the best utensil, as it prevents scorching. The proportion of grain to water must be learned from experience, but as a general rule the cereals require from two to four cups of water to one of grain.

All cereals need thorough cooking. The time is long for some; two to three hours in the double boiler is not too long for oats or coarse wheat. The flavors are then developed, and the starch granules are thoroughly cooked and easy to digest.
modern_explanation:
Cereals are the foundation of the human diet across cultures, and Kinne and Cooley explain why with elegant simplicity: they're cheap, rich in starch and protein, and can feed a family. Their explanation of what happens to starch granules in boiling water—they swell, burst, and release their contents, thickening the liquid—is the food science behind every porridge, sauce, and pudding.

The authors note something important: cereals need thorough cooking, not just a quick heat-up. Two to three hours for oats in a double boiler might sound extreme compared to modern five-minute oats, but they're describing the steel-cut or whole-grain cereals common in 1914. The long cooking time genuinely develops flavor and ensures the starch is fully cooked. The fireless cooker (an insulated box) was their answer to the fuel cost of this long cooking.

Their description of toast science—how dry heat converts starch to dextrin, giving it a sweeter, brown flavor before it continues to pure carbon (burning)—explains why good toast has that characteristic nutty flavor. This chemistry is still used today in breadmaking discussions and explains the Maillard reaction that makes browned foods so appealing.

Modern quick oats and instant cereals were developed precisely to address the long cooking times described here. Pre-steamed and rolled flat, they hydrate faster but sacrifice some texture and flavor compared to the slow-cooked original.
key_takeaways: Starch granules must swell and burst in boiling water to fully cook—this is why cereals thicken liquid.
A double boiler prevents scorching during the long cooking required for whole-grain cereals.
Two to three hours of cooking in a double boiler develops the best flavor in whole-grain porridges.
Toast flavor comes from converting starch to dextrin through dry heat—one step before burning.
Use two to four cups of water per cup of grain as a starting proportion, adjusting by experience.

title: Salads and Desserts: Their Place in the Menu
source_book: Foods and Household Management: A Textbook of the Household Arts
original_text:
=Salads.=——Salads have an important place in the menu because they supply raw food materials, because they are appetizing, and because they often include foods not otherwise provided.

Salad greens——lettuce, watercress, chicory, escarole, dandelion——and celery belong in the group of raw food materials that we should use freely and daily. The daily use of greens is so important that the housekeeper who cannot have them as a regular salad might well serve them in some other form. The proteins, fat, and carbohydrates in salad dressing are valuable food materials, and there is at present a tendency to reduce the amount used in the hope of decreasing the total fuel value of the diet.

=Desserts.=——There are people who are not satisfied without a sweet at the end of the meal. Some feel that a meal is incomplete without a dessert, and it has a social value that cannot be easily measured. We have developed a love of sweets that is somewhat excessive, and with this has come the custom of making elaborate dishes for this final course.

A dessert of fresh fruit is simple and wholesome. An ice or sherbet is refreshing and if made of fruit is not excessive. Stewed fruit, baked apple, or apple sauce are good. Custard is valuable. Gelatine desserts are well liked. Plain cake, plain cookies, and gingerbread are wholesome and satisfying. Rich cake, rich pastry, hot cake or doughnuts, and fried cakes are not easily digested when taken at the close of a full meal. Ice cream is wholesome, but should be taken at the end of the meal that it may not chill the stomach before the food is digested.
modern_explanation:
This chapter captures something enduring about the structure of meals: salads and desserts have psychological and social value beyond their nutrition. Kinne and Cooley acknowledge this gracefully—a meal is a social event, not just a nutritional transaction.

Their advocacy for daily raw greens—lettuce, watercress, chicory, escarole, dandelion—is now validated by decades of nutritional research. Cruciferous and leafy greens contain fiber, vitamins C and K, folate, and phytochemicals with anti-inflammatory properties. The authors were advocating for this before the science fully backed them up.

The dessert hierarchy they propose is still sensible: fresh fruit > ice/sherbet > custard/gelatine > plain cake > rich pastry/fried cakes. This corresponds reasonably well to modern nutritional ranking by glycemic impact, fat content, and fiber. Rich, fried, or very sweet desserts at the end of an already full meal genuinely do challenge digestion—the fat slows gastric emptying significantly.

Their observation that Americans have 'developed a love of sweets that is somewhat excessive' is not only still true but has intensified dramatically—added sugar consumption has roughly doubled since 1914.
key_takeaways: Daily raw greens (lettuce, watercress, dandelion) are so important that they should be served in some form at every meal.
Fresh fruit is the best dessert; ice and custard are acceptable; rich pastry and fried cakes are hardest to digest after a full meal.
Ice cream should be served at the end of a meal, not the beginning, to avoid chilling the stomach before digestion.
Salad dressings add protein, fat, and carbohydrate—valuable nutritionally, though excess fat can be reduced.
Dessert has social value as well as nutritional value—a simple, wholesome sweet is perfectly appropriate.
